summary: O'Neil argues that while black box algorithms are increasingly used as tools of social control against teachers and civilians (particularly young men of color), there's a glaring absence of similar accountability mechanisms for police. She notes that existing police metrics like CompStat focus only on arrests and tickets, ignoring data on police violence. The post was prompted by the Tamir Rice case, where a prosecutor refused to 'second-guess' officers who killed a 12-year-old. O'Neil contends that our priorities are backwards when we scrutinize teachers more than police who kill children.
key_points: Black box algorithms function as tools of social control, primarily targeting teachers (Value-Added Models) and civilians (predictive policing)
Police accountability metrics like CompStat measure only arrests and tickets, not violence or injuries caused by officers
Data on police shootings and injuries isn't formally analyzed, possibly not even collected systematically
Society invests heavily in identifying 'bad teachers' while doing little to identify dangerous police officers
The Tamir Rice case exemplifies the lack of accountability, with prosecutors explicitly refusing to 'second-guess' police
money_quotes: How black box algorithms have become tools of social control.
What's missing here? Why haven't we built, for example, models which assess police?
How about how many people the policeman has shot or injured? As far as I know, that data isn't analyzed, if it's even formally collected.
I do think our country has got its priorities backwards when we put so much focus and money towards getting rid of the worst teachers but we do very little towards getting rid of the worst cops.
The prosecutor was quoted as saying, 'We don't second-guess police officers.' I maintain that we should do exactly that.
We should collect and analyze data around police actions as long as children are getting killed.
key_insight: If we're going to use algorithmic tools of social control against teachers and civilians, intellectual consistency demands we also build accountability systems that scrutinize police behavior—especially their use of violence.
